narrative: Dear [Student's Name], imagine the admissions team looking at your application as a story of your journey toward becoming a lawyer, piecing together the chapters from your undergrad days through law school to predict if you'd pass the bar on the first try. Your story starts strong with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.3 and an LSAT score of 25.5, but the model saw some early hurdles that shaped its view. The biggest chapter influencing this prediction was your Year 3 decile of 2, placing you in the lower ranks among your peers, which strongly pulled the outlook toward a tougher road ahead—more so than anything else. Right behind that, your LSAT score stood out as another key factor tipping toward higher risk of not passing. Your Year 1 decile of 2 echoed that early pattern, reinforcing the sense of consistent challenges in keeping pace academically. Coming from a middle-income family added to this picture, as the model noted it as a moderate influence toward struggle compared to higher brackets. Your Hispanic background and being male played smaller but similar roles in painting this cautious portrait. Even your undergraduate GPA of 3.3 nudged things that way, though your choice to go full-time offered the one bright counterpoint, gently pulling toward success. Together, these elements built a narrative where the probability of bar failure landed at 44%, enough for the model to recommend holding off on admission to protect your future options. It's not the end of your story—you've got real strengths here, like that UGPA and full-time commitment, that could shine brighter with targeted growth in areas like exam performance. This is just one data-driven snapshot; your determination can rewrite the next chapters. Keep pushing forward.
